title: Problem 1
statement_text: Does there for every integer $d \geq 0$ exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $d$-degenerate graph with an assignment of lists of size $d + 1$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Theorem 2 shows that lists of size $d+2$ suffice for weighted $\varepsilon$-flexibility of $d$-degenerate graphs. The question is whether one extra color (size $d+1$, matching choosability) also guarantees flexibility. For $d=0$ trivial; for $d=1$ (forests with lists of size 2) weighted $1/2$-flexibility is easy; the first open case is $d=2$.
notes: PDF source; math is simple enough to read cleanly.

title: Problem 2
statement_text: Does there for every integer $\Delta \geq 2$ exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every connected graph of maximum degree $\Delta$ that is not $\Delta$-regular, with an assignment of lists of size $\Delta$, is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: This is a Brooks'-theorem-like analogue of Problem 1: Brooks' theorem says connected non-$\Delta$-regular graphs of maximum degree $\Delta$ are $\Delta$-choosable, so the question asks whether such graphs are also $\varepsilon$-flexible with lists of size exactly $\Delta$.

title: Problem 3
statement_text: Does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar graph with an assignment of lists of size $5$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Planar graphs are 5-choosable (Thomassen), so 5 is the tight choosability threshold. Theorem 4 currently gives $\varepsilon$-flexibility only for lists of size 6. The authors believe these bounds can be improved.

title: Problem 4
statement_text: Does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar graph of girth at least $5$ with an assignment of lists of size $3$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Planar graphs of girth at least 5 are 3-choosable (Thomassen). Theorem 5 (and Theorem 4) imply $\varepsilon$-flexibility with lists of size 4; the question is whether the choosability-tight list size of 3 also yields flexibility.

title: Problem 5
statement_text: Does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar triangle-free graph with an assignment of lists of size $4$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Euler's formula implies planar triangle-free graphs are 4-choosable. The paper notes this as a simpler-seeming but still nontrivial instance of the flexibility question at the choosability-tight list size.

title: Problem 6
statement_text: Does there exist $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every planar graph of girth at least $6$ with an assignment of lists of size $3$ is weighted $\varepsilon$-flexible? Or at least $\varepsilon$-flexible?
context_text: Euler's formula implies planar graphs of girth at least 6 are 3-choosable. Theorem 5 gives weighted $\varepsilon$-flexibility for planar graphs of girth at least 12 with lists of size 3; Problem 6 asks whether girth 6 suffices.
